reason: Both answers correctly identify this as high existential risk, and both cover the same core actions: remote config for fast patching, breakage telemetry, synthetic monitoring, resilient selector strategies, diversifying acquisition channels, capturing users into a direct relationship via accounts/email, and checking whether the official API could reduce exposure. Neither has factual errors. A adds a useful distinction between survivable DOM breakage and catastrophic Chrome Web Store delisting; B adds more concrete engineering specifics (stable custom element tags, MutationObservers for SPA navigation, ARIA roles) and a concrete expected-breakage-frequency estimate. These differences roughly offset and neither changes the asker's next move more than the other.
